era_context: 340 BCE was a year of escalating conflict and political tension across the Mediterranean and Asia. Philip II of Macedon was at the height of his power and was preparing for his final push to dominate Greece. Artaxerxes III was aging and facing challenges to Persian authority. In China, Qi was experiencing internal instability while other Warring States powers were consolidating their strength. This year represents a moment of transition before the major conflicts that would reshape the Mediterranean world in the following years.

events.title: Demosthenes delivers Third Philippic against Philip II
events.description: Demosthenes delivered the Third Philippic, his most famous speech against Philip II of Macedon. The speech was a passionate and eloquent call for Athenian resistance to Macedonian expansion and for Greek unity against the Macedonian threat. The Third Philippic became an iconic expression of Greek resistance to foreign domination and remains one of the most celebrated speeches in Western history. However, despite its rhetorical power, the speech failed to mobilize sufficient Greek opposition to Philip, and Athens remained divided and unable to effectively counter Macedonian expansion.

sources.name: Demosthenes, Third Philippic (primary text, speech delivered 340 BCE)

events.certainty_note: The Third Philippic is preserved as a primary text and is generally dated to 340 BCE. The date is well-established in ancient sources.

events.title: Philip II besieges Perinthus; conflict with Athens escalates
events.description: Philip II besieged the city of Perinthus (in Thrace), which was allied with Athens. The siege was a major military operation and a direct challenge to Athenian interests. The conflict over Perinthus escalated tensions between Philip and Athens and demonstrated Philip's willingness to directly confront Athenian power. The siege lasted several months and was ultimately unsuccessful, but it marked a major escalation in the conflict between Philip and the Greek city-states.
key_figures: Philip II of Macedon (King, r. -359 to -336)

sources.name: Diodorus Siculus, Bibliotheca Historica, Book XVI
Demosthenes, Third Philippic and other speeches

events.certainty_note: The siege of Perinthus is well-attested in ancient sources and is generally dated to 340 BCE. The basic facts of the siege are not disputed.
cross_references: Part of Philip's expansion into Greek affairs

events.title: Artaxerxes III dies; succession crisis in Persian Empire
events.description: Artaxerxes III Ochus died in 340 BCE (or possibly 338 BCE, sources differ), creating a succession crisis in the Persian Empire. The death of the aging Great King, who had spent his reign consolidating Persian power and suppressing rebellions, left the empire vulnerable to internal instability and external threats. The succession was contested, and the empire would experience a period of internal conflict and weakening before the arrival of Alexander the Great.
key_figures: Artaxerxes III Ochus (Persian Great King, r. -358 to -338 or -340)

sources.name: Diodorus Siculus, Bibliotheca Historica, Book XVI

events.certainty_note: The death of Artaxerxes III is attested in ancient sources, but the exact date is uncertain. Some sources place it in 338 BCE, others in 340 BCE. The dating is approximate within ±2 years.
cross_references: Precedes the succession of Arses (-338-001) or Darius III (-336-001)
Marks the beginning of the Persian Empire's decline

events.title: Qi state experiences military setback; decline accelerates
events.description: The state of Qi experienced a significant military setback, possibly against the state of Zhao or another rival power. This defeat marked the beginning of Qi's rapid decline from its position as one of the dominant Warring States powers. The military setback, combined with internal political instability, would lead to Qi's loss of power and influence in the following years.

sources.name: Shiji (Records of the Grand Historian) by Sima Qian

events.certainty_note: The military setback of Qi is attested in Chinese sources, but the exact date and nature of the conflict are uncertain. The year 340 BCE is conventional; the setback may have occurred in 339 or 341 BCE.
cross_references: Part of Qi's decline from dominance
Precedes Qi's loss of power in the 320s

disconfirming_evidence: The date of Artaxerxes III's death is disputed; some sources place it in 338 BCE rather than 340 BCE. The exact nature and outcome of the siege of Perinthus are debated; some sources suggest it was more successful for Philip than others indicate. The characterization of Qi's military setback in 340 BCE is speculative; the sources do not provide a precise date or detailed account of the conflict. The significance of Demosthenes' Third Philippic is sometimes overstated; while it is a famous speech, it did not immediately mobilize Greek opposition to Philip.
historiographic_note: The year 340 BCE is moderately well-documented for the Mediterranean world through Greek sources, particularly Demosthenes' speeches and later historical accounts. However, these sources are biased toward Greek perspectives and often hostile to Persian interests. Egyptian sources are sparse. Chinese sources provide information about Warring States conflicts but are themselves compilations with editorial biases. The Indian subcontinent, Sub-Saharan Africa, and the Americas are absent from surviving records. The historical record reflects the preservation bias of literate, centralized states with written traditions.

graph_edges.note: Demosthenes' Third Philippic in 340 BCE contributed to the mobilization of Greek opposition that led to the Battle of Chaeronea in 338 BCE
The siege of Perinthus in 340 BCE escalated tensions that led to the Battle of Chaeronea in 338 BCE
The death of Artaxerxes III in 340 BCE created a succession crisis that weakened the Persian Empire before Alexander's invasion
Qi's military setback in 340 BCE contributed to its rapid decline in the 320s
